video_title: SemiAnalysis, Altimeter, Nebius, Glean.. 12 Hot Takes From Biggest Names in AI
gist: Rapid-fire 'hot take' interviews from the Raise Summit in Paris with 12 AI founders/investors (Applied Intuition, SemiAnalysis, Nebius, Glean, Altimeter, Turbo Puffer, Wonderful, Legora, Merge, Navan, NYSE, and a data-layer exec). Recurring threads: enterprises shifting from land-grab to cost/ROI scrutiny, an open-source-vs-closed model inflection, memory-cost inflation lifting token prices, a live debate on whether 'token maxing' still pays, and consumption-based pricing spreading beyond coding into legal and other verticals.

insights.claim: Applied Intuition frames its mission as putting one AI model onto a billion machines across cars, trucks, defense ships (Huntington Ingalls), and Heidelberg Materials quarries/mines/ports, betting a single trained model can generalize across many machine types.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: A cross-vertical physical-AI platform is a differentiated bet vs pure-LLM players; watch the promised 'biggest announcements in company history' as catalysts.

insights.claim: Applied Intuition says it has barely spent its raised capital and has 1,000+ engineers, positioning it to deploy 'multiple billions' toward any single opportunity while insisting on reconciling model spend with customer revenue rather than spend-now-monetize-later.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: A well-capitalized private with revenue discipline is better insulated than cash-burning peers if AI funding tightens.

insights.claim: Physical-AI adoption diffuses more slowly and linearly than LLMs because safety and real-world machines prevent the boom-bust spikes and rapid new-entrant churn seen in software AI.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: Implies steadier, more defensible revenue in physical AI but slower TAM capture; different risk/return profile than software AI names.

insights.claim: The current AI cost structure (model-training spend plus corporate 'opulence') marks a late-bubble phase, and a pullback is coming faster than the typical long boom-bust timeline because cycles now move much quicker.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: A framework for timing risk: don't assume a multi-year runway before any correction; compression of cycles argues for earlier de-risking.
insights.quote: we're we're at that phase of the bubble where it's like you know you know like the all the opulence in the companies is what people talk about rather than the products

insights.claim: The AI/compute buildout resembles LTCM: an elite, over-leveraged system where a peripheral shock could cascade through interconnected AI businesses via a domino effect.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: Flags systemic/contagion risk in AI-infra financing (leverage on compute) as the non-obvious tail risk to monitor, not demand.
insights.quote: could there be an LTCM situation in our business where you have something that's so elite and hopeful and then suddenly like some things don't work and it stumbles

insights.claim: Many buyers are over-optimizing three-year AI data-center infrastructure against a backward-looking snapshot of today's models, which will leave a lot of that custom-built infra sub-optimal or wasted once model workloads shift in two years.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Argues for flexible/general-purpose compute over bespoke optimization; a warning on stranded-capex risk for hyperscalers and neoclouds.
insights.quote: it feels like a lot of people are trying to optimize on on the current rather than like think about where their workload is heading

insights.claim: Software-hardware co-design is necessary but dangerous when you can't predict where models are headed, creating a bind where both over-committing and under-committing to co-design produce non-optimized infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Nuance for evaluating chip/system startups: co-design is a double-edged moat, not a guaranteed advantage.

insights.claim: France has spare nuclear power that attracted data-center plans, but Spain, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands and Germany pushed back because that spare French power is being sold to keep their grids stable.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Cross-border grid dependency is a real siting constraint on European data-center growth beyond local regulation.

insights.claim: Nebius operates 20 data centers as a diversified portfolio, arguing that competitors relying on single large projects are dangerously 'single-threaded' against local regulatory, community and power risks.
insights.speaker: Mark (Nebius)
insights.so_what: Portfolio breadth is Nebius's stated differentiator vs single-site neoclouds; a lens for judging execution risk in the sector.

insights.claim: Nebius is guiding to 'tens of billions' in revenue and expanding from model-training capacity into inferencing and agentic workloads as enterprise adoption becomes the next growth stage.
insights.speaker: Mark (Nebius)
insights.so_what: Signals the revenue trajectory and product shift (training to inference/agents) that underpins the Nebius bull case.

insights.claim: Multiple Chinese model labs are telling inference providers their next models will be licensed rather than open-sourced, which the SemiAnalysis view reads as open source 'dying quickly.'
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Directly contradicts the consensus that open weights keep winning; watch Chinese labs' licensing shift as a business-model inflection.
insights.quote: there's multiple Chinese model labs who are telling all the inference guys, hey yeah, our next model is not going to be open source. We're going to license it to you.

insights.claim: Glean's founder predicts open-source models will dominate AI inferencing within two years, flipping from almost no open source today to almost all inference running on open source.
insights.speaker: Arvin (Glean)
insights.so_what: A directly opposing forecast to SemiAnalysis in the same episode; the open-vs-closed inference split is genuinely contested among insiders.
insights.quote: open-source models are going to dominate AI inferencing. You know, you'll see in the next 2 years, we'll shift to where's almost no open source to where it's going to be almost all open source.

insights.claim: Altimeter cites Decagon's Jesse that roughly 90% of Decagon's model volume now runs through open source, with frontier closed models reserved for discovering new use cases and coding.
insights.speaker: Apoorv (Altimeter)
insights.so_what: Concrete data point that production volume already skews open-source while frontier spend concentrates on coding/new use cases.

insights.claim: Rising memory costs are being passed through the stack: B200/B300 server pricing is up year-over-year, next-gen hardware has taken price increases before production even starts, and token costs are no longer falling as fast as before.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Memory-cost inflation could halt the deflation in inference/token prices markets assume, pressuring AI-app gross margins.
insights.quote: We've seen next generation hardware receive price increases before they've even started production.

insights.claim: SemiAnalysis argues token budgeting is 'loser mentality' and anyone who doesn't 'token max' will be left behind, because clamping usage stops employees learning new AI workflows that drive more work with fewer people.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: One side of an explicit intra-episode debate on whether maximizing token spend still yields ROI.
insights.quote: I think anyone who doesn't token max is going to get left behind. I think all this uh token budgeting stuff is is loser mentality.

insights.claim: Merge's CTO takes the opposite view that token maxing no longer works, with more tokens delivering 'zero results,' and the only real productivity link is using AI to shorten cycle times and iterate faster.
insights.speaker: Gil (Merge, CTO)
insights.so_what: The bear side of the token-maxing debate; suggests usage-based AI ROI is plateauing, relevant to model-vendor revenue durability.
insights.quote: Token maxing not working. You're getting zero results from using more tokens.

insights.claim: A team member unknowingly spent ~$1,000 running Claude Code on Haiku (thinking the tool was 'mid') before switching to Opus/Fable, illustrating that model selection, not raw token volume, drives productivity.
insights.speaker: Dylan Patel
insights.so_what: Anecdotal signal that mis-set default models waste spend; model-tier routing materially affects unit economics.

insights.claim: Altimeter's framework is 'four seasons in AI' — OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX and Google — and CIOs making multi-hundred-million-dollar bets should 'plan for the climate, not the weather,' meaning multimodel routing, evals and post-trained custom models rather than locking into one lab.
insights.speaker: Apoorv (Altimeter)
insights.so_what: A memorable enterprise-procurement heuristic; tailwind for routing/eval/model-management vendors and against single-lab lock-in.
insights.quote: there's four seasons in AI. They're called OpenAI, Anthropic, SpaceX, and Google.

insights.claim: Altimeter notes SpaceX (via Michael) just launched a model at Opus level and OpenAI's Saul went live the same day, treating xAI/SpaceX as a genuine fourth frontier lab alongside OpenAI, Anthropic and Google.
insights.speaker: Apoorv (Altimeter)
insights.so_what: Positions SpaceX/xAI as reaching frontier parity, reshaping the competitive map of who enterprises route to.

insights.claim: Turbo Puffer powers retrieval for Cursor, Notion, Lovable and Anthropic, and enables web-scale vector search by building on cheap object storage (S3/GCS) plus a heavy caching layer that recovers near in-memory performance.
insights.speaker: Naquille (Turbo Puffer)
insights.so_what: Object-storage-plus-caching is the architectural moat lowering retrieval cost; names a marquee customer roster for the category.

insights.claim: Turbo Puffer's largest workloads already cost tens of millions of dollars to search over tens of terabytes, and customers now want to search hundreds of petabytes, which would add one to two orders of magnitude in cost.
insights.speaker: Naquille (Turbo Puffer)
insights.so_what: Search/retrieval is a large and fast-growing cost center in agentic apps; cost-per-query reduction unlocks new products and margins.
insights.quote: our largest workloads cost tens of millions of dollars to search over right now and those are webcale use cases with tens of terabytes.

insights.claim: Turbo Puffer argues search is still an order of magnitude too expensive, and cutting per-user search cost from ~$5 to ~50 cents would make previously-uneconomic products viable and unlock explosive growth.
insights.speaker: Naquille (Turbo Puffer)
insights.so_what: Frames search cost as the gating factor on a wave of new AI products; watch retrieval pricing as a leading indicator of app-layer TAM.

insights.claim: Glean positions its context graph as an ROI lever: it reduces enterprise token usage by routing tasks to the cheapest capable model (across closed and open) and by delivering assembled context in one shot so models spend less compute gathering raw materials.
insights.speaker: Arvin (Glean)
insights.so_what: Ties Glean's value directly to cost reduction as enterprises start measuring AI spend, a durable buying trigger.

insights.claim: A data-layer exec argues 'data is back' as the unsung hero, with any AI application only as good as its data layer, making the foundational data layer the key dependency for agentic apps.
insights.speaker: guest, data-layer exec (CJ)
insights.so_what: Reinforces investment thesis in data-platform layers as agent adoption scales; data quality as the constraint on AI performance.
insights.quote: data is the unsung hero and data is back.

insights.claim: Wonderful's contrarian bet is that geographies will matter more than verticals in AI: a horizontal, generalist enterprise-AI product can win by focusing on rest-of-world (non-US) markets with local delivery, run as an Uber-style country-by-country land grab.
insights.speaker: Barack (Wonderful, CSO)
insights.so_what: A non-consensus GTM thesis; geographic-first expansion (30+ countries in 6 months) is the differentiation, not vertical depth.
insights.quote: I believe geographies will be even bigger than verticals when it comes to AI.

insights.claim: Legora raised $600 million in two weeks and became the first legal-AI company to move to consumption-based pricing, arguing per-seat flat rates break down when a user consumes multiples of the subscription in tokens.
insights.speaker: Max (Legora)
insights.so_what: Consumption pricing is migrating from coding tools into legal and other verticals; a template shift for vertical-SaaS margins.
insights.quote: typically you don't raise $600 million in two weeks.

insights.claim: Legora runs positive gross margins by aligning consumption-based pricing with token costs and expects most vertical AI companies to follow coding tools and the big labs into usage-based models.
insights.speaker: Max (Legora)
insights.so_what: Flat-rate vertical AI SaaS faces margin risk as token usage rises; usage-based pricing is the emerging defensive standard.

insights.claim: Merge's CTO argues integrations are where an LLM becomes dangerous because that is the point data can flow out, so enterprises will demand governance, AI sovereignty and the ability to switch providers before opening AI to all their systems.
insights.speaker: Gil (Merge, CTO)
insights.so_what: Security/sovereignty gates enterprise AI rollout; tailwind for governance, on-prem/portable inference and anti-lock-in tooling.
insights.quote: integrations are the point where an LLM actually becomes dangerous.

insights.claim: Navan reports growing usage 50% and revenue 40% last quarter, turning cash-flow positive and profitable with over $10 billion in annual bookings roughly nine months after going public.
insights.speaker: Ariel (Navan)
insights.so_what: Hard post-IPO KPIs for a profitable AI-era travel platform; a benchmark for AI-vertical apps reaching profitability.
insights.quote: we have more than 10 billion dollars of bookings a year now

insights.claim: Navan built its own model and platform to prevent hallucination in travel because errors (wrong flights, wrong rooms) are emotionally intolerable to customers, arguing humans remain essential and you 'cannot have an AI agent do everything.'
insights.speaker: Ariel (Navan)
insights.so_what: High-stakes verticals require custom anti-hallucination models and human-in-the-loop, a limiter on full agent automation.

themes.theme: Enterprise buying shift to cost & ROI

insights.claim: Across the summit, enterprise AI buying is shifting from 12-months-ago 'give me everything and I'll take five of it' abundance to hard budget/cost-consciousness and demands to measure spend, which will pressure both IPO-scale incumbents and niche startups.
insights.speaker: guest, Applied Intuition founder (Kasar)
insights.so_what: The 'reconciliation' from experimentation to ROI scrutiny is the macro theme; favors cost-reducing tools and squeezes undifferentiated startups.
insights.quote: budgets and cost consciousness is becoming like a more core theme
